speaker: interviewer
text: --describing life in Sankt Michael, the-- the 23 Jews that were there at this little place in Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was administering it? And-- and I had asked you about whether the SS came.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --the-- I know that the mayor of the-- the Sankt Michael, or a surround-- I don't know how big this was. Because we were only in the farm. He came almost every day. And I think the Smith family was responsible for us. But there were often-- Germans were coming, and they knew exactly where we are. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What was-- what was the daily life like, and what do you remember about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Daily life was hungry. And-- and there was a-- there was a Mr. Smith's-- Mrs. Smith's sister, I think-- I believe. I'm not sure if the sister or the sister-in-law. She had a daughter. And this daughter came after-- in there-- not in-- in the end of, sort of. And she was there for awhile, but she wasn't there right away. And she had a son, Hansy. His name was Hansy. And he was dressed nicely. And they must have known already that they are losing the war. Because at one time, from out of the blue, he showed a picture of a man. And he was in uniform. And he says, oh. This is her husband. And he's not an SS, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- but we liked, my brother and I, we liked Hansy very much. Why? Because Hansy was given-- he was a skinny little boy, blond boy. He was-- and I don't know how old he was, but he was a young boy-- probably our age-- or surround-- or around that age. He was given a slice of bread. Now, can you imagine to a hungry person-- a slice of bread covered thickly with butter, and on the butter, thickly honey.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the mother says, don't let the children take it from you. But we've-- we-- we took. We-- how did we-- didn't-- we didn't take it away. Because then meanwhile, the mother was looking out the window was there. It was-- you could see.

speaker: interviewer
text: To make sure he didn't give it to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't. But she couldn't-- didn't look all the time, and he was always playing with us. There was front of the-- of the house, like a triangle, and most beautiful green lawn. It looked to me a perfect triangle-- the mountain up there, and this, the foot of the mountain, this triangle. And between, in the middle of the triangle, there was a huge apple tree-- the most beautiful apples you can imagine-- big red apples. My mother was craving for this apple. I always try to shake it-- didn't wanna come down. But once it came. And what we did-- Hansy was there, and we were already waiting, waiting. He's coming. Sometimes he took a long time before he came out. He came out, and we heard the mother says, now, don't let those [NON-ENGLISH]-- bad children take it from you. But she wasn't really mean to us. And the Smith family was-- I mean, they were decent to us. They didn't hurt us. They were just-- we were working there for them. And she says-- and she let him out, the little boy. So we says, come. Come here. By this time, we were talking German. He was coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we said, you know, we are going to have a game. What kind of game? She says. So I said, the game is going to be like this. We are all going to lie down on the grass, and we are going to roll.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you are in the middle. And my brother is going to roll to you. And I'm from this side to you. And whoever gets to you first has to take a bite from the bread. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got us some food this way. And then the-- and then we told him, don't you dare tell your mother. Don't you dare tell your mother what we did this. But because 1, 2. 3, the slice of bread was gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- he-- he liked the game very much. I wonder if he remembers it. He liked the game very much. And he was fascinated. So he didn't eat too much. But he had no problem. He just went back. And we says, don't you dare tell your mother. Of course, a child, he told the mother. He comes back-- [LAUGHS] --again with a bread. And this time she's watching. But this happened a few times. We could give-- get the bread from him like this with a game. And again, sometimes the mother came out and says, you bad children. You-- I know you are tricking him. And you're just taking the food away from him. But I guess she didn't mind it so much, because she saw that we are hungry, and-- and end of the war, and showing this picture. So probably, you know, we should have a good impression, you know, something like that. I-- I'm just speculating. So they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the people in the house talking or hearing any news? Or were there ever any radios or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A matter of fact, they-- the-- our leader, who was-- we picked, you know, a leader of the Jewish. Group. He is the one who was-- somebody was talking, somebody came. He's the one who was-- they were talking to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This was the [NON-ENGLISH]. We called him the [NON-ENGLISH], a Weinberger. Mr. Weinberger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was a--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he the doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was an inmate also. He was a concentration camp inmate. He was also in the concentration camp. And he had a-- a Christian wife. He-- she wasn't there, but he was in the concentration. And I think his-- they were all mixed-- most-- a lot of mixed marriages were there. But only the Jewish partner was in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they getting news?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he, when the mayor-- I don't know. But the mayor liked to come and talk to him. You see, he was-- he-- he must have been an educated man. And he must have been interesting, because he came in. I-- I know that he was talking. And I know that I didn't have my yellow star. And every time when I-- I saw the mayor is there, I wouldn't dare to talk to him, because he didn't talk to none of us-- just with him. And I just went like this. I covered myself here. Because I have-- I was afraid if he-- he-- if I won't have the yellow star, he will, you know-- he won't like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- the-- the Mr. Weinberger, sometimes he says, you know, they are losing the war. They are losing the war. So must have been the mayor who was telling him this, or something. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he-- he says, they are losing. They are losing. So we were hopeful maybe they will come. They will come.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --it come a time that some Germans were coming. There were some people. They saw us there, went away, and then a paper came with the mayor-- with the paper came. And he says-- he says-- he has it here that the 23 Jews in this farm has to be put against the wall on so and so date, and they have to be shot through the head.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --the mayor said, well, if you can do something to save yourself, I won't know what you're doing. I won't know anything. Don't tell me, and I don't know. Something like this, he said.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did the Jewish people do? Well, the men, they were doing it. They went up into the mountain-- went up on the mountain. And there was a lot of trees. It were like a forest, some parts.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they dug a-- a big hole, a deep hole. It wasn't so wide, and it wasn't so long. Because we were, you know-- it wasn't so comfortable there. And it took a little while. But they were working against time, so because the date was put out there. And if the German promise that they will come, they will come.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we went into this. We called it the bunker-- like a bunker-- a bunker. And it was the raw earth. Raw earth, and we had a little-- about this wide, maybe-- for my mother and the four children. And this wide, and perhaps maybe this long for five of us to sit there.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother got very sick with her stomach. And we had some little food, which Mrs. Smith gave before we went down there. She came upstairs, and she brought a little bit of flour. And she says, do what you want with it for the children. And my mother did something-- I don't know-- little cookies-- I don't know-- with-- from nothing, you know-- with flour. And there wasn't really much. And there was a little bit of cookie. And my mother, says, oh, I have the cookie. And I cannot eat now, because my stomach is so bad. And, of course, there is no washroom. It's just a ditch-- black ditch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she says, oh. When I get better, I'm going to have the cookie. But what happened? But don't imagine a cookie, a beautiful cookie. This was pieces of little-- you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my mother made a terrible, big mistake, because she put this little bundle of cookies between my brother and me. And my brother and me, each time our hand met-- because we didn't see each other. It was very dark. It was in the earth. We were covered. It was-- we went down. And in a ladder would put-- was put down. And we went down. And on the top, the Smith family, they closed that, and they put earth on it. And they put leaves-- dirt and leaves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let me just understand. All the 23 people went, when the-- just before the day--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --into this bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right-- what we build ourselves. We were there. And-- but going back to the cookie-- every time-- [LAUGHS] --we met, our hands met-- my younger brother and mine-- our hands met always in this little bag. And the other says, this is the last one. We are not going to take anything no more, because mother has-- [LAUGHS] --you know, needs it. So we ate it all. Selfishly, we ate it all. The hunger is so terrible, it hurts. It's that your body's in pain. Really, I think the mind is already numb, and the body is in pain. It's sort of a dizziness. It's very painful. Hunger is very painful. And then my mother, after a few days, she got better. And she said, let me see. In the dark, she says, you have it, Ilonka, you, and Tibor, you have it there, the cookies. Give it to me. Then the guilt came. I felt terrible guilty. I gave her the empty little-- little sack there. And she says, you, you ate it all. You didn't leave me one cookie. I felt so guilty. She didn't say anything more. Nothing more was said about it. She only used to mention if I know already when we were-- or right after the war, and we had plenty of food. She says, you ate the cookies. [LAUGHTER] But not-- not-- not when-- when-- not-- not ever-- she didn't mention it. She knew how hungry we were. But I was very guilty, terrible guilty. And my brother also says he feels very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you any idea how long you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there for eight days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eight days and nights?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nights, eight days and--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the complete dark?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the complete dark. Sometime, one of the ladies-- and I can't remember which one it is-- a woman. She started to scream. I need light! I need light! And she screamed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were people-- I think the [NON-ENGLISH] had some match. And she striked-- he striked one match. So how long does a match burn? That's how long the light was on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, very stern, now listen. I can't afford too many matches. But if you get crazy like this again, we have to do something about you, because you are going to give us away. Because we hear people walking. People were going, you know, because this mountain, the people were living up on the mountain. There were houses up there high, different places. And people passed by. And some of them were coming right over us. And we heard them you know, walking when they came to that little closing part. Because this was a little bit wooden.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, you are-- we can't afford it-- that if you endanger the rest of the people. You have to shut up. And then she went for hours. Once in a while, she's screaming. And then a match went up.

speaker: interviewee
text: But every day, the Smith family, or one daughter, or the other daughter-- they were growing girls-- brought us in two pails, coffee-- coffee. And then they were bringing one day two pails of boiled in the skin potatoes. So that was lovely, but very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were the only people that knew that you were there-- just the Smith family?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were the only-- Smith family and-- and must have been. The mayor, I don't know. But he says we should do what we want. And they wouldn't. But what happened middle of this ordeal, because this was terrible to be alive in the earth, raw earth, and the clothes was very shabby, what we had-- just really hardly any. We had just rags on ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, very excitedly, one of the daughters, Smith daughters, comes. She says, the-- the-- the hole-- she called it the hole-- is for rotten. This-- it-- it's spoop. It's-- it-- it's-- it's already out. It's-- it's not a secret anymore. Something like this, it means. What happened? His father-- her father, I mean, her father went to the tavern. He got drunk. And he started to talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and when he came home, they noticed. So he says, come on, out. And I don't know what. I don't know what you should do now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we are coming-- this is daytime. And we are coming out from the mountain. I don't think we-- I don't know if we were so scared or what. We were numb already. I think we were numb.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when we are walking in this-- in-- in the sky, and the sun, somehow, the will was there again. And I started to get scared again. I got scared again, because when I look down from the mountain-- now, we are quite a bit high there. And you can see the farm. And then you can see how you go out, and then the little bridge, wooden bridge. And then you can see the road. And on the road is full of cars, such a-- army trucks, and different kind of trucks, and even, I think, a carriage, horse and such-- of buggies, and full of Germans, full of German soldiers in uniform. And they are-- some of them are walking. Some of them are sitting on those vehicles. And I started to get very scared. And I started to pull my mother's skirt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, mother, I am very scared. And where is father? And where is father? So she says-- and then she told me. I tell you, don't be scared. Father is at home. He-- he's waiting for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's when I got this picture that he's holding the knob and he wants to get in. And I said, but he hasn't got a key. And she says, don't worry. He will get in. And I'm very scared. And we are walking. And the whole group is very sad. And because we thought this is going to be the end now. Then we stopped. And-- and the [NON-ENGLISH] girl is with us. And then suddenly, we stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we started to-- the grownups are talking. And they are discussing it-- to go back to the farm. So we are going to be dead. They are going to come and get us. They are going to shoot us. So what should they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They decided they are going to go back to the hole, to the bunker. And whatever comes, comes. But this is the only thing we can do. And we'll go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we walked back. And-- and we stayed there till the-- the Russians, the Russian came. And the Russian came, and they fed us. How did-- how did they find you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just-- we-- it was-- it was nighttime. They-- it wasn't nighttime. They were coming in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was a captain-- a good-looking-- wasn't very tall, but strong man, with a Russian uniform, with this hat. And he started to say-- because there were lot of more-- more people were coming-- more Russians were coming. But he was the captain. And he says, where is the judenlager-- the Jewish lager? So we says, this is the Jewish lager. And who is the-- who can he talk to? So are you, poor Mr. Weinberger.

speaker: interviewee
text: But Mr. Weinberger only spoke German. And this captain didn't speak German. He spoke Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he tries to talk. Nobody understands him. And he says, anybody speaks Yiddish here? He says, I cannot understand. He says, it's a Jewish group, and you don't speak Yiddish. What kind of Jews are you if you don't speak Yiddish? So he says, anybody? Nobody can understand Yiddish? So my-- we were scared. We don't know. We don't know if he was friendly or not. So my mother says, I speak Yiddish. So she-- he says, you speak Yiddish? And he sat down, and-- to the table. He says, you sit right next to me, to the left-- left side. I remember. And I sat next to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, you will translate. So first he was giving that not to speak Yiddish is unforgivable. [LAUGHS] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was Jewish. He was a Jewish Russian captain-- the Russian army. He claimed that he was [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know which [NON-ENGLISH]. But this is what he said.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother says, if-- I-- I-- I-- I-- if you are [NON-ENGLISH] then I have to give you a big respect. So there was, right away, he liked my mother that not just that she speaks Yiddish, but she gives him respect-- because who he is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then he was telling us not to be so afraid. And he's-- he's a Jew himself. And we are free now. And we are going to be all right. So-- Do you remember when that was? Was it the summer yet of '45?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were-- we were freed in-- what was the date? What is the freeing date?

speaker: interviewer
text: April or May?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been in May, not in April, because we got back in-- in June, or July, we got back. I-- no we were back in-- it was hot. We got back in-- must have been in July, we got back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell me what happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: June or July.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from the liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what has happened, what this-- when the Russians came in-- and then it was a nighttime, I remember. And myself and my siblings, we were all sleeping. My sister and  two brothers were sleeping. And he says, he wants to see there. No, I'm sorry. They came in at nighttime. And they came in at nighttime. Because he says he wants to see this lager.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he says, where is it? And he had a flashlight. He had it in his hand. And he was flashing it on us children, and well, always the baby were dressed half-naked, you know, in bed. And he says, let me check this. This is also a-- a-- a Yiddish lager? So the children were-- and then-- what was your question? Now what happened from the time of the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. What happened? When they came in the next day, the [NON-ENGLISH] were-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: How are you? The [NON-ENGLISH] were very scared, because they had these two young girls, the Smiths. And then they had this-- this one-- this one of the woman had a daughter-- the daughter there. And they were very scared. So they asked us if we would stay there with them for a little while, just to tell the people who come, the Russians who were coming, and whoever else comes, to tell them that they were fair to us. And they didn't hurt us. And-- and that was fair to do, because they did not hurt us.

speaker: interviewee
text: RECORDING (ANSWERING MACHINE): Hiram-- Hiram Kurtz, it's 5:30. Oh. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-oh. It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the family were very scared of the Russians, obviously, so they asked you to stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the family, yes. They asked that we should stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: To vouch for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the two girls and this other daughter, this relative, they went down to this-- where we were. And they were there for a little while. And, of course, we were bringing up food, and the family was bringing food. They were afraid because, you know, the soldiers, you know. I guess you know the stories. They were girls, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --so they wanted to be safe. And the were asking if there are any young people or anything. And-- and-- and we said, there are nobody, just you, just the Smith family, those people who the-- the older people who are there. And so we stayed with them. Well, I don't know if it's one week or two weeks we stayed-- we were staying with them. And I think it was fair, because they're-- the Smith family, they did not hurt us. They did not hurt us. And I remember after that, we said goodbye to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you allowed to go back? How did you go back to Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went-- we went to the train. And this Russian captain gave us a paper, a note, where it says you will have the train-- one car to yourselves. And nobody else will go back there. Just you will sit there. And-- and if anybody wants to come there, you just show this paper. He signed it. And for a while, for a few days, it was good. And then we were going. Because the train, you know, didn't just go all the time. It was-- it took a long time to get home. I don't know how long, but also, it seems a long time. It also was standing, the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have to go to any other camps or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: --DP camps or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. We-- we didn't move. We went straight back to Budapest. And then we were shown this car. And after a while, after a few days, more Russia soldiers says, in Russian, because I think one of-- a few guys, a few of the men understood Russian there. And he says, oh, heck with this-- what Jewish people want-- tore the paper apart and just-- you just should have come here, the rest of it. And they were sitting there. And we were kicked out. We had to get off. That was finished. So-- and there were-- the rest of the train, there was such a-- also, this was a good part of the train, you know, with seats and everything. There were cattle trains, a lot of trains there, the same train. And there were a lot of soldiers. And-- and in this part already, everybody was going on their own way. So my mother and the children are alone. We aren't-- we have nobody there anymore. I don't remember anybody there anymore. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was the five of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Five of us. And those solders are there. And they are laughing, the Russian soldiers. And-- and we are saying that we wanna go up, up to the train. And they were saying in Russian, Jews! And they were from the train, kicking, like to go away. So they didn't let us. And then my next picture what I see in my head again, that we are sitting-- I don't know how we got on there-- it's the same train or some other one. But we are not sitting on the train. We are sitting-- you know how you connect the trains together, those big things, how you-- I don't know how you call those-- almost like pipes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Locks or what-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Locks, and it has a flat part. And they used to-- you know, they go together, and you chain them together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- we are sitting on that. On that we arrived to Budapest. That's how I arrived at Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: You arrived in Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest--

speaker: interviewer
text: And from Budapest to-- to back home, further East?

speaker: interviewee
text: Budapest we arrived, and there a man came. And he started to touch us-- head, hand. And he started to talk. My mother says, he's crazy. Don't talk to him. He was talking. He says, he was told that no children are survived. None of the children are survived, and he hasn't seen any children yet. And he came back from somewhere terrible. I don't know which part of the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he's looking for his family. If he has a lot children, also. And he says, if we are alive, and we came back-- he says, from where to? From Austria. Then he has hope his children will come back. And he started this. At first he says, are you really living children? He was asking, are you really alive? And my mother says, don't talk to him. He must be crazy.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know that he's in Auschwitz. And he was offering us some money. And my mother didn't take it. He was a-- she was-- we were afraid. We didn't know what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: But all the way-- and he says, he told us where to go. And they are going to feed us there. So they were already waiting for people who were coming back. And he said, the Joint must have been-- you heard about the Joint? This is like a-- Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was already, I remember they gave out--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were setting up, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --soup. They gave us such a nice soup there. It was delicious. And I wanted to eat more. And I really couldn't eat more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you?

speaker: interviewee
text: A big plate--

speaker: interviewer
text: Got sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: --and I started to eat and eat. And after a few spoons, I put it down. I couldn't eat. And I was-- I was so sorry. I couldn't eat it. I just-- I was very hungry, but I couldn't eat. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: This stage you knew already things were over and better. As a child, what-- what were you feeling at that time then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was feeling-- I was scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still scared.

speaker: interviewee
text: Scared because-- never been to Budapest before. It's a big city. We were walking there. Nobody was waiting for us, just this man was saying, real children, are you still really alive? Then, I-- I can't remember how we got back from Budapest, but it must have been on a train. We reached Hajdunanas. We went back to Hajdunanas for the only reason, because we truly believed, but my mother says, because she herself believed, that then our father is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we arrive.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nobody-- nobody's there, nobody. One uncle from my father's side, one brother from my father's side, he's there. He's the only one, nobody else. And we came back. We went to the Safar house-- empty, just the walls, no furniture, no food. The Hungarian, when they saw us, they said, they remark, more Jews came back than went away. Not even a glass of water they would offer us.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I'm almost finished, I hope.
